content:
First, note that  
\[
S \;=\; 9 + 9^2 + \cdots + 9^{2010}
      \;=\;\frac{9\bigl(9^{2010}-1\bigr)}{8}
      \;=\;\frac{9^{2011}-9}{8}
\]  
is an integer.  We check small primes \(p\) in increasing order:

1. \(p=2\).  Each \(9^k\) is odd, so \(S\) is a sum of \(2010\) odd terms, hence even.  Thus \(2\mid S\).  
2. \(p=3\).  Since \(9\equiv0\pmod3\), every term vanishes modulo 3, so \(3\mid S\).  
3. \(p=5\).  Mod 5, \(9\equiv4\).  The powers of 4 mod 5 cycle: \(4,1,4,1,\dots\) of period 2, and \(2010\) is even, so the sum is \(0\pmod5\).  
4. \(p=7\).  Mod 7, \(9\equiv2\).  The powers of 2 mod 7 cycle with period 3 and each full block sums to \(2+4+1=7\equiv0\).  Since \(2010\) is divisible by 3, \(7\mid S\).  
5. \(p=11\).  Mod 11, \(9\) has order 5, and one full 5‐term cycle sums to \(0\pmod{11}\).  Since \(2010\) is a multiple of 5, \(11\mid S\).  
6. \(p=13\).  Mod 13, \(9\) has order 3, each 3‐term cycle sums to \(0\pmod{13}\), and \(2010\) is a multiple of 3, so \(13\mid S\).  

Next prime is \(17\).  We check
\[
9\equiv9\pmod{17}, 
\quad
9^2\equiv81\equiv13\pmod{17},
\]
so the first two terms give \(9+13=22\equiv5\pmod{17}\).  In fact one finds
\[
\sum_{k=1}^{2010}9^k\;\equiv\;5\;\not\equiv0\pmod{17},
\]
hence \(17\nmid S\).  Therefore the smallest prime not dividing \(S\) is

\[
\boxed{17}.
\]
